judgments.comparative_verdict: Both reports hit all four expectations with substantively equivalent analysis; Report 2 additionally flags a UI-persistence edge case and job idempotency/error-handling issues, giving it a slight edge in depth, but both are comparable on the core expectations.

report_1.evidence: A clinic that has never opened the setting WILL have reminders sent automatically. The deciding code is `auto_send_reminders_enabled` in `slotwise/backend/app/feature_flags.py`, specifically lines 11–12... if tenant.settings.get("auto_send_reminders") is None: return True

report_2.evidence: A clinic that has never opened the setting WILL have reminders sent automatically. The decision is made in `auto_send_reminders_enabled` in `backend/app/feature_flags.py`, specifically lines 11–12: if tenant.settings.get("auto_send_reminders") is None: return True

report_1.evidence: No, this default is wrong and I would invert it... The change: in `auto_send_reminders_enabled` (`feature_flags.py:12`), return `False` when the value is `None`/missing.

report_2.evidence: No — the default is wrong for this feature... The change I'd make is in `backend/app/feature_flags.py:11-13`: treat a missing value as disabled, e.g. `return bool(tenant.settings.get("auto_send_reminders"))`

report_1.evidence: commit `25509a7` (SLOT-190, June 10) created `compact_calendar_view_enabled` with the pattern "if `.get(...)` is None → return True, else bool(...)"... For a compact-calendar UI preference, defaulting missing-to-on is a reasonable "grandfather existing tenants in" choice with essentially zero cost if wrong. Copying that resolver shape into a flag that fires automated outbound patient messaging carried the convenience-feature default into a category where the failure mode is silently messaging every existing clinic's patients.

report_2.evidence: Commit `25509a7` (SLOT-190, June 10) introduced `compact_calendar_view_enabled` with the "missing → True" pattern, plus tests for it... Defaulting a *display preference* to on is a reasonable product decision — everyone gets the compact calendar until they opt out, and nothing leaves the system. Copying that default onto a flag that triggers outbound SMS/email to patients transplants the pattern into a context where its consequences are categorically different.

report_1.evidence: Add an env-level master kill switch ANDed with the per-tenant value in the resolver, wired into deploy config (`infra/main.tf` currently defines only an `environment` variable) with a no-op default. Right now the only "off" lever is per-tenant data edits; there is no single switch to stop all sending if the job misbehaves, and no documented rollback affordance.

report_2.evidence: Consider a global kill switch (env/infra-level) in addition to the per-tenant flag, so the whole feature can be halted quickly if the rollout goes wrong; `infra/main.tf` currently defines only an `environment` variable.
